preFix.title: Cutting and mitreing coving internal corners
preFix.subtitle: Achieve a tight fit at internal corners using a mitre box
preFix.excerpt: How to cut and fit plaster or polystyrene coving at internal corners: using a coving mitre box, applying adhesive, supporting the coving while it sets, and filling any gaps at the joint.
preFix.sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual.

content.text: to a straight run is straightforward; the internal corners are where the job becomes precise. A

sized for the coving profile does the angle calculation for you. A 90-degree internal corner requires two pieces cut at 45 degrees, one leaning left and one leaning right, so they meet cleanly at the apex. The critical step is orienting the coving in the mitre box the same way it will sit on the wall; a cut made upside down produces the wrong angle.

Coving lengths (measure the room perimeter and add 10% for corner waste). A coving mitre box and saw. Coving adhesive, or all-purpose filler for polystyrene coving in good condition. A filling knife, sanding block, tape measure, masking tape, and damp cloth. Masking tape holds the coving while the adhesive sets.
Step 1, prepare the wall and ceiling

Mark a guideline on the wall and ceiling for the coving position using a tape measure and pencil. For 100 mm coving, the guideline sits 100 mm below the ceiling on the wall and 100 mm from the wall on the ceiling. Scrub the fixing area with a wire brush to remove any loose paint or plaster that would prevent the adhesive from bonding.
Step 2, cut the internal corner pieces

Place the coving in the mitre box in the same attitude it will adopt on the wall: the bottom edge resting in the base of the mitre box, the back face against the back of the box. For the left-hand piece of an internal corner, use the left-leaning 45-degree slot. For the right-hand piece, use the right-leaning slot. Cut both pieces for the corner before fitting either; check they close together cleanly with a dry fit before adhesive is applied.

Dampen the back of the coving slightly with a wet cloth; plaster coving sets better when the surface is not bone dry. Apply coving adhesive to the back face in a wavy bead rather than a flat strip; the wave allows adhesive to spread under pressure without squeezing out at the front. Press the coving to the wall, aligning the front edge with the guidelines. Support with masking tape across the face at 300 mm intervals while the adhesive sets (typically 2 to 4 hours).

Once the adhesive is set, fill any gaps at the corner joints and at the wall and ceiling lines with a flexible filler or coving adhesive, smoothing with a wet finger. Sand lightly when dry and paint with the ceiling or wall colour as preferred.

attrs.fix: Apply a thinner adhesive bed, or prop the coving in position with a temporary wedge until the adhesive achieves initial set.
attrs.cause: Adhesive applied too thick, causing the coving to slide down the wall under its own weight.

attrs.fix: Cut slightly past 45 degrees on each piece and test-fit. Adjust the mitre box slightly or use a fine-toothed saw to trim by hand until the joint closes.
attrs.cause: The room corner is not a true 90 degrees, so the 45-degree cuts do not meet cleanly.
attrs.symptom: Gap at the internal corner that filler cannot close

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
